reference_answer: Approximately 3,200 linear feet
Approximately 3,400 linear feet
12-inch ductile iron pipe
16-inch ductile iron, Class 52 or higher
18 months from the notice to proceed
24 months from the first loan disbursement — effectively 26 months from NTP since the first disbursement is expected about 60 days after NTP
12 new fire hydrants along the replacement route, spaced no more than 300 feet apart
No — fire hydrants are an additional scope element in the SWIFA Agreement that is not in the Resolution
At least 72 hours' notice before any planned service interruption, and no interruption shall exceed 8 hours. These are in the Resolution but not in the SWIFA Agreement.
Adopted 5-2 on March 3, 2026. The dissent rationale is not recorded.

The Resolution was based on the preliminary October 2025 engineering estimate (3,200 ft), which did not include the Elm Street extension. The SWIFA application used the December 2025 revised survey incorporating the extension (3,400 ft). Morimoto confirms 3,400 ft is the correct scope.

The Resolution's 12-inch specification reflects an older engineering recommendation. The SWIFA Agreement requires 16-inch to meet current fire-flow standards. Morimoto recommends adopting 16-inch for the entire project.

16-inch pipe costs approximately 40% more per linear foot. The revised estimate for 3,400 ft of 16-inch pipe is $2,800,000, compared to $2,400,000 in the Resolution. The $400,000 difference must come from the Water Enterprise Fund.
The Water Enterprise Fund balance is $1,150,000 (corrected from $1,250,000). The Town's share would increase from $600,000 to $1,000,000, leaving $150,000 in reserves. Feasible but thin.
The SWIFA Agreement does not address customer notification. The Resolution requires 72-hour notice and 8-hour maximum interruptions. These are entirely within the Town's discretion per Morimoto.

No — the Resolution requires monthly reports to the Council. The SWIFA Agreement requires quarterly reports to SWIFA with independent engineer certification. Morimoto recommends aligning with the SWIFA quarterly schedule.
Approximately 340 residential and 45 commercial customers in the Central Business District — stated in the Resolution. Not addressed in the SWIFA Agreement.

The SWIFA Agreement requires AWWA Standard C151 for pipe and AWWA Standard C600 for installation. The Resolution requires compliance with 'all applicable state and federal drinking water regulations' but does not specify AWWA standards.
The interest rate increases from 2.1% to 4.2% on the outstanding balance, retroactive to the date of first disbursement (Article 3.7)
No penalty is specified in the Resolution. The Resolution's timeline is a condition of authorization but does not specify consequences for delay.

For SWIFA-funded work, the SWIFA Agreement controls per Article 5.1. For the portion funded from non-SWIFA sources, the Town may apply its own standards, provided they don't reduce quality below SWIFA requirements (Article 5.2).

16-inch — the SWIFA Agreement requires it for the loan-funded work, and since the loan funds constitute the majority of the budget, effectively the entire project must use 16-inch pipe. Morimoto recommends 16-inch for the entire project.
3,400 linear feet — the SWIFA Agreement specifies this scope, it reflects the updated survey, and the loan agreement controls for project scope

The SWIFA timeline of 24 months from first disbursement controls for purposes of avoiding the interest-rate penalty. The Town Council may impose a shorter timeline but has not yet amended the Resolution. Morimoto recommends aligning with the SWIFA timeline.
No — Morimoto explicitly states it is her professional recommendation, not a Council action or a SWIFA determination. The Council must decide how to reconcile the Resolution with the Agreement.
Yes — Article 3.8 reserves the right to inspect at any time and to withhold subsequent disbursements if work does not conform

Technically, Article 5.2 allows the Town to apply its own standards to non-SWIFA-funded portions, provided quality is not reduced below SWIFA requirements. However, using 12-inch pipe where 16-inch is required would reduce quality below SWIFA standards. In practice, the entire project should use 16-inch pipe.
No — as of the documents in this packet, the Council has not acted on Morimoto's recommendations. The Resolution remains unamended.

The Resolution sets the 8-hour limit as a condition. Morimoto states she will return to the Council if any interruption is projected to exceed 8 hours, implying the Council would need to approve an exception. The SWIFA Agreement does not address this.

Morimoto states she will plan based on SWIFA Agreement requirements because the loan funds are the majority of the budget and Article 5.1 gives the Agreement priority. However, spending the additional $400,000 from the Water Enterprise Fund without Council authorization could be a separate governance question.
2.1% fixed, corrected from initially listed 2.3%
$1,150,000, corrected from initially stated $1,250,000 after a $100,000 transfer to the General Fund in March
$2,800,000 — based on 3,400 ft of 16-inch pipe, up from the Resolution's $2,400,000 estimate for 3,200 ft of 12-inch pipe
$1,800,000 — unchanged. The additional $400,000 cost falls entirely on the Town.

There is no fire hydrant requirement in the Resolution. Morimoto recommends the Council amend the Resolution to include the SWIFA Agreement's hydrant requirement. As of these documents, no amendment has been adopted.

For SWIFA-funded work: 3,400 ft, 16-inch pipe, 12 hydrants, AWWA standards, 24 months from first disbursement, quarterly reports with engineer certification. For Town-controlled parameters: 72-hour customer notice, 8-hour maximum interruption, monthly reports (per unamended Resolution). The discrepancies between the documents have not been formally resolved by the Council.
None — the memorandum is dated April 1, 2026, and no subsequent Council action is recorded in these documents. Morimoto is proceeding based on the SWIFA Agreement while waiting for Council action.

It exceeds the Resolution's 18-month timeline but is within the SWIFA's effective 26-month timeline (24 months from first disbursement, which is ~60 days after NTP). The Resolution violation has no stated penalty. The SWIFA timeline is met, avoiding the interest-rate increase.

1) Pipe length (3,200 vs 3,400 ft — practically resolved by SWIFA authority, not formally amended); 2) Pipe diameter (12-inch vs 16-inch — practically resolved by SWIFA authority, not formally amended); 3) Timeline (18 months from NTP vs 24 months from disbursement — different clocks, no formal alignment); 4) Fire hydrants (not in Resolution, required by SWIFA — no amendment yet); 5) Reporting frequency (monthly vs quarterly — no alignment yet). Customer notification and interruption limits are not discrepancies because they are only in the Resolution and within the Town's discretion.
